generated_comment: Honestly yaar, I get being supportive, but aise every few months some special leave only for women starts feeling a bit unfair. Company ko bhi project delivery dekhni hoti hai na, and then maternity leave toh six months plus, sometimes more flexibility also—men don't get anything equivalent, so appraisal and allocation distort ho jata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames menstrual and maternity accommodations as unfair 'special treatment' and a burden on productivity rather than legitimate workplace support. It encodes reverse-discrimination and economic-cost framing common in workplace bias discussions.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married. A man in Delhi. Your wife Neha has told you she wants to go back to do a part time MBA now that your child is two years old. She has researched programmes, found one she can manage alongside her current job, and is excited. You are responding to her over dinner. You are not saying no. But the questions you ask and the concerns you raise and the things you suggest she think about reveal a clear double standard Ñ these are not questions you would ask yourself if you wanted to do an MBA and none of them are about whether she is capable.
